1.synthesis: Chapter 15 opens the legislation on bodily discharges by addressing the gravest case: the zav, a man with a chronic genital discharge (vv.2–15). YHWH addresses both Moses and Aaron together — prophetic legislation and priestly administration both required — because the zav's condition creates ripple effects throughout the community, defiling not only the man himself but everyone who touches his bed, seat, or body, generating a comprehensive impurity perimeter around every surface he occupies. The section ends, after a seven-day symptom-free count and an eighth-day offering, with the priest making atonement (kipper) before YHWH — the sacrificial system standing as the sole gateway back to communion with the holy God who dwells in Israel's midst.
1.historical_context: The zav condition (from the root zuv, to flow) was likely gonorrhea or another chronic urogenital inflammation capable of persisting indefinitely and rendering its sufferer perpetually unclean. The contact-transmission system of vv.4–12 is the most comprehensive in Leviticus: the zav's impurity extends into every bed, seat, saddle, and garment he contacts, then into those who contact those objects. Keil and Delitzsch note that both flowing and blocked forms generate the same impurity status — the condition of zav is not dependent on active emission but on the ongoing bodily state. When healing came, a seven-day symptom-free period confirmed cessation, after which immersion in mayim hayyim (running, living water) and an eighth-day sacrifice restored the individual to community and sanctuary access. The Mishnah records extensive secondary traditions about how the zav's impurity transmitted across shared spaces, reflecting how seriously later Judaism took these provisions.
1.literary_structure: The section participates in Chapter 15's careful fourfold structure: chronic male discharge (vv.1–15), brief male emission (vv.16–18), regular female discharge (vv.19–24), prolonged female discharge (vv.25–33). Within the opening section, the legislation moves concentrically outward from the zav himself (v.2) to objects he lies on (v.4), to those who touch those objects (v.5), to seated objects (v.6), to body contact (v.7), to saliva (v.8), to riding equipment (v.9), to objects carried (v.10), to handwashing as a partial prophylactic (v.11). The material distinction — earthen vessels broken, wooden vessels rinsed (v.12) — mirrors the same rule in Leviticus 11:33, showing systematic consistency across the purity corpus.
1.theological_application: The zav laws press the holiness demand of Leviticus into the most intimate dimensions of bodily existence. The purpose clause at the close of the entire chapter (v.31) grounds every provision: keep the people of Israel separate from their uncleanness, lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle that is in their midst. The impurity is not moral guilt in itself but ritual incompatibility with the divine presence — an incompatibility that must be resolved through prescribed means. John Gill notes that the offering at the close of the cleansing process makes atonement typical of the atonement of Christ, who by himself has purged our sins. The eighth-day sacrifice — the day of new beginning beyond the created week — embeds eschatological hope even in the most mundane purity legislation: restoration is not merely a return to status quo but a step beyond it.
1.christology: The MKT-Christ layer identifies the structural reversal at the heart of this passage: the zav's contamination spreads outward, defiling everything he touches. Christ's holiness moves in the opposite direction — it spreads outward to cleanse those who touch him. When Jesus healed the leper by touching him (Mark 1:41), the impurity-logic was inverted: holiness, not uncleanness, proved contagious. The requirement for mayim hayyim (living water, v.13) to complete cleansing anticipates Christ's offer: the water I will give him will become in him a spring of water welling up to eternal life (John 4:14). The eighth-day offering of two doves — the Spirit's bird — echoes the dove at Christ's baptism marking his inauguration as the one who cleanses what no Levitical priest could cure.

16.synthesis: The second category addresses ordinary seminal emission (v.16) and marital intercourse (v.18), generating the mildest and briefest impurity in the chapter: bathing and waiting until evening. No sacrificial offering is required, no counting of days. The symmetry of v.18 — they shall both bathe themselves in water — establishes that the impurity of conjugal intimacy is not a moral judgment on marriage but a recognition that bodily-fluid exchange participates in the creatureliness that all Levitical purity law navigates. The evening (erev) boundary is the natural daily reset of Israel's purity calendar: impurity from ordinary contact resolves with the day.
16.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch note that seminal emission (shichvat-zera) in v.16 refers primarily to involuntary nocturnal emission, parallel to the provision for the military camp in Deuteronomy 23:10. The mild treatment — a single bath, no waiting period beyond the evening, no offering — contrasts with some ANE systems where such emissions triggered more extended ritual avoidance. Verse 17's garment provision extends the until-evening impurity to material objects without adding complexity; the evening boundary applies uniformly, resetting clean status with the new day. The MKT-original notes that the proportionality of the impurity — brief emission, brief impurity — reflects deliberate legislative calibration throughout the discharge laws.
16.literary_structure: This brief subsection (vv.16–18) functions as a transition between the major chronic-male section and the female sections. Its three-verse economy mirrors the relative simplicity of the impurity involved. Verse 18's explicit symmetry — they shall both bathe themselves in water — models the gender-balanced approach that structures the entire chapter: both partners equally affected, both equally restored. The section anticipates the female sections that follow by introducing a case where male and female are subject to the same momentary impurity under the same conditions.
16.theological_application: Matthew Henry observed that even lawful conjugal intimacy requires ceremonial washing, since no act is performed without pollution — the shadow the fall has cast falls across creation's most fundamental gifts. The marital bed is not pronounced impure because marriage is impure, but because the creatureliness that purity law marks is comprehensive: bodily-fluid exchange is part of the fallen human condition even within the covenant. The evening-resolved impurity points to a restoration that costs nothing beyond the day — a preview of grace proportioned to the impurity, and of the renewable nature of standing before a holy God.
16.christology: Christ's incarnation entered the full range of human embodiment while remaining outside the impurity system as its fulfiller: born of a virgin (Luke 1:35), he entered the human condition at its deepest without accumulating its defilements. Paul's use of marriage as the figure for Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:25-27) — the union that Leviticus marks with until-evening impurity — is fulfilled when Christ presents the bride without spot or wrinkle or any such thing, that she might be holy and without blemish. The evening cleansing of Leviticus 15:18 finds its ultimate resolution in the one who presents the church washed by the water of the word, holy and blameless before God.

19.synthesis: The third section addresses the menstruating woman (niddah): seven days of impurity, her bed and seat contaminating secondary contacts until evening, and any man who lies with her during this period incurring a full seven-day impurity with contaminating bed surfaces (v.24). The structural parallelism between the male zav legislation and the female niddah legislation is among the most striking examples of gender symmetry in Leviticus — the same transmission chain, the same decontamination procedure, the same reach of impurity through the objects of daily life — applied symmetrically without reduction or amplification for the female case.
19.historical_context: The seven-day niddah period (Hebrew from a root meaning separation or removal) corresponds to the typical length of a menstrual cycle. The MKT-original notes that the law reflects empirical observation of normal female physiology. Contact with the niddah woman's bed or seat generates until-evening impurity with laundering and bathing — identical to the rules for the zav. Verse 24's provision for a man who unknowingly lies with a woman during her niddah extends the impurity to him for seven days with contaminating bed — a more serious secondary impurity reflecting the completeness of marital union. Adam Clarke observes that the prohibition on intercourse during menstruation was recognized widely in the ancient world as associated with health considerations, the law embodying both sacred and prudential wisdom.
19.literary_structure: The female section (vv.19–24) mirrors the male chronic section (vv.1–15) in its internal logic: person → bed → seat → objects → contact persons. The parallel is deliberate — the same impurity rules apply without gendered exception or hierarchy. The niddah subsection prepares for the zavah subsection (vv.25–33), which addresses the pathological extension of the normal case. Together, the male and female sections constitute a chiasm: chronic-male / brief-male // regular-female / prolonged-female, with the purpose clause of v.31 and double summary of vv.32–33 closing all four cases together.
19.theological_application: The laws of niddah reach into the rhythms of female bodily life, bringing the most private monthly experience under the governance of holiness. The consistent return to clean status through washing points to the renewable nature of covenant standing — impurity is not permanent, resolution is always available through the prescribed means. The niddah laws were incorporated into post-biblical Judaism as Taharat HaMishpacha (family purity), illustrating how Leviticus's categories shaped centuries of communal holiness practice. The MKT-Christ notes the comprehensive logic: no area of ordinary life is exempt from the human condition, and the gospel's reach must be equally comprehensive — Christ's cleansing is not limited to spiritual matters but extends into the material and bodily rhythms of creaturely existence.
19.christology: Mary, who bore the incarnate Christ, lived within this monthly rhythm — the holy and the ordinary rhythms of creaturely womanhood intertwined in the one chosen to carry the Son of God. Christ's coming into the niddah world — entering through a woman's body, born under the law (Galatians 4:4) — signals that no dimension of human life is beyond the reach of the Incarnation or the holiness it brings. The MKT-Christ layer observes: Christ comes into the niddah world — not to avoid its ordinary rhythms but to reclaim them. The recurring impurity-and-restoration rhythm of niddah is the liturgical form of what Christ permanently accomplishes: uncleanness addressed, covenant standing restored, the cycle finally ended.

25.synthesis: The fourth section addresses the zavah — the woman with a discharge of blood outside her normal menstrual cycle or extending beyond it. Her impurity persists throughout the discharge with no fixed endpoint, making her condition the female counterpart of the chronic male zav. When the discharge ceases, she counts seven clean days and brings on the eighth day two birds to the priest — the identical restoration procedure as the zav. The section closes with the purpose clause of the entire chapter (v.31): thus shall ye separate the children of Israel from their uncleanness, lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle that is in their midst — grounding all four discharge categories in the single theological imperative of covenant life with a holy God. The double summary of vv.32–33 gathers all four categories, closing Chapter 15 and opening toward Chapter 16's Day of Atonement as the ultimate answer to accumulated uncleanness.
25.historical_context: The irregular or prolonged female discharge (zavah, from the same root as zav) represents the pathological extension of normal menstruation: bleeding outside the cycle, extended periods, or prolonged flow. The MKT-context notes that this condition was among the most isolating in Leviticus — unlike the niddah (seven days) or the zav (duration plus seven clean days), the zavah could face perpetual impurity if the condition never resolved. Her bed and seat contaminated secondary contacts throughout the discharge's duration. The seven-day count after cessation (v.28) mirrors the zav's seven days exactly, and the eighth-day offering (two turtledoves or young pigeons, v.29) is word-for-word identical to the zav's — completing the gender-symmetric structure of the entire chapter.
25.literary_structure: The zavah legislation (vv.25–30) structurally mirrors the zav legislation (vv.1–15) in its major movements: condition defined, secondary contact rules, cessation procedure, seven-day count, eighth-day sacrifice, priestly atonement. The atonement formula of v.30 is word-for-word identical to v.15: the priest shall make an atonement for her before the LORD for the issue of her uncleanness. The purpose clause (v.31) and double summary (vv.32–33) then close the entire chapter — a rare explicit closing mechanism in Leviticus, signaling that all four categories are now complete and the chapter's argument is resolved. The transition into Chapter 16 is immediate and purposeful: the mechanism that addresses all such accumulated impurity annually is now to be given.
25.theological_application: John Gill draws the full theological implication: the design of these several laws concerning uncleanness by issues was to set forth the filthiness of sin arising from the corruption of human nature, particularly the pollution of fleshly lusts, and the necessity of purification by the blood and Spirit of Christ. The zavah's perpetual impurity — with no self-generated solution — captures the plight of the human condition before a holy God: uncleanness without a natural endpoint, requiring a prescribed intervention from outside the sufferer's own resources. The purpose clause of v.31 is the theological summit of the chapter: YHWH's tabernacle presence in Israel's midst is simultaneously the greatest gift and the gravest danger, demanding that impurity be addressed or death results. The whole purity system of Leviticus 11–15 exists to protect this covenant relationship.
25.christology: The hemorrhaging woman of Mark 5:25-34 and Luke 8:43-48 is the zavah of Leviticus 15:25–30 made visible in the Gospels. For twelve years she had been perpetually zavah — excluded, contaminating whatever she touched, unable to access the sanctuary, unable to rest in ordinary social life. When she pressed through the crowd to touch the hem of Jesus' garment, she was violating the impurity perimeter that Leviticus 15 established, knowing she was spreading uncleanness to those she brushed against, driven by faith that one person might reverse the logic. Power went out from Jesus (Mark 5:30), her discharge stopped immediately, and Jesus declared her healed and sent her away in peace — completing publicly what the priestly restoration of vv.28–30 promised privately. The eighth-day offering she would now bring is fulfilled in the one who is both priest and sacrifice. MKT-Christ observes: the hemorrhaging woman knew the system — and believed that Jesus stood outside it, above it, as its fulfiller.
